nativeRange.habitat: Green Star Polyp inhabits shallow tropical reefs, lagoon rubble zones, and reef flats where water movement is moderate and light penetration is good. It is commonly found growing alongside other encrusting soft corals such as Xenia and Clavularia.

decorPreferences: isolated frag rock or island rock (strongly recommended to prevent mat from spreading to main rockwork)
back wall or overflow box placement
sand-moat barrier around dedicated GSP rock

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via symbiotic zooxanthellae; does not require supplemental feeding. Optional target feeding of phytoplankton or amino acid supplements is sometimes reported to enhance coloration and growth rate, but most colonies thrive with light alone.

goodWith: fast-growing soft corals with adequate spacing
avoidWith: slow-growing SPS corals (Acropora, Montipora) — GSP mat can overgrow and smother them
expensive LPS corals in proximity — at risk of encroachment
fleshy soft corals adjacent to GSP colony edges
any coral placed on connected rockwork without sand or glue barrier — GSP will spread across it

summary: Green Star Polyp is a fast-growing encrusting soft coral prized for its ease of care and vivid green polyps. Its most notorious trait is aggressive spreading — GSP can overtake an entire reef tank if not isolated on a dedicated rock from day one.
careNotes:
RUNAWAY-SPREAD WARNING — the single most important GSP husbandry consideration: Green Star Polyp is widely regarded as among the most aggressive spreaders in the reef hobby. Its purple or reddish stoloniferous mat (the creeping base tissue that anchors and extends the colony) will colonize any hard surface it contacts — live rock, glass, frag plugs, other corals, and even PVC equipment — expanding at up to 2–3 cm per month under good conditions. Many experienced reefers have come to regret introducing GSP to their main rockwork, because complete removal is extremely difficult: any fragment of mat left behind tends to regenerate into a new colony. The mat bonds tightly to porous live rock surfaces and generally cannot be peeled cleanly.

Containment strategies (use from day one, not after the fact):
• Place GSP exclusively on an isolated rock or frag disc surrounded by a sand moat (minimum 5 cm of sand gap — the mat generally will not cross open sand).
• Alternatively mount it on the back glass or overflow box where spread is more manageable.
• Super glue or epoxy borders can slow lateral expansion but require regular maintenance.
• Aggressive LPS corals (Torch, Frogspawn, Hammer) along perimeter edges may sting back advancing GSP mat, but this is not a reliable containment method.
• If GSP escapes to main rockwork, manual removal with a razor blade is typically the only option — expect lengthy labor and incomplete results.

Taxonomic note: This species was originally described as Clavularia violacea in the early nineteenth century, later transferred to Pachyclavularia violacea, and reclassified as Briareum violaceum following a 2016 systematic revision based on sclerite analysis. Both older names remain in circulation in the aquarium trade and hobby literature. The Indo-Pacific encrusting form is distinct from the Caribbean stalked and branching form Briareum asbestinum, known in the trade as Corky Sea Finger, which has different husbandry requirements and is documented separately.

Lighting and flow: GSP tolerates a wide range of conditions (50–200 PAR) and has been reported to do well under most modern reef lighting. Direct high-intensity laminar flow can cause chronic polyp retraction; moderate indirect turbulent flow is generally preferred. Polyps that stay retracted for extended periods (days to weeks) are usually signaling a water chemistry issue or direct flow irritation.

Polyp extension cycles: Healthy GSP typically extends its polyps during the photoperiod and retracts at night. Brief closure (hours to a few days) after a water change or introduction is normal. Prolonged closure (weeks) without obvious water-chemistry cause can indicate allelopathic interference from neighboring corals, particularly Sinularia or Sarcophyton species.

Fragging: GSP propagates readily by cutting sections of mat and securing them to a new substrate with super glue gel or rubber bands on a frag plug. New frags generally extend polyps within 24–72 hours once settled.
breedingNotes: Green Star Polyp reproduces vegetatively via stolon expansion — the outward creep of its encrusting mat — which is the primary mode of both natural colony growth and captive fragging. Sexual reproduction through broadcast spawning occurs on natural reefs but is not practiced or documented in home aquaria. Fragging is straightforward: cut a section of mat, attach it to a frag plug with super glue gel or netting, and place it in moderate flow. Frags typically begin extending polyps within one to three days.

primary.name: LiveAquaria – Metallic Green Starburst Polyp
primary.notes: Used for primary care parameters (temperature 72–78°F, SG 1.020–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, kH 8–12, lighting moderate-to-high, flow medium-to-strong, care level easy, feeding notes). Designated Briareum sp. (formerly Clavularia viridis in listing text).

additional.name: ReefBay – Complete GSP Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters (75–80°F / 24–27°C, SG 1.024–1.026, pH 8.1–8.4, kH 8–12, Ca 380–450 ppm, Mg 1250–1350 ppm), PAR 50–350 (optimal 100–250), care level beginner, spread risk, and containment strategies.

additional.name: Extreme Corals – GSP Care Requirements
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature 76–80°F, SG 1.024–1.026, pH 8.1–8.4, kH 8–10, Ca 400–450 ppm, Mg 1250–1350 ppm, PAR 50–150. Confirmed isolated-rock placement recommendation.

additional.name: Saltwater Aquarium Blog – GSP Coral Care
additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific name Pachyclavularia violacea, order Alcyonacea, temperature 78–82°F, SG 1.025, pH 8.2, kH 8–12, spreading behavior detail (colonizes any hard surface including other corals and glass). Confirmed easy care level.

additional.name: WoRMS – Briareum violaceum (taxonomic authority)
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy: family Briareidae, order Scleralcyonacea (=Alcyonacea), synonyms Clavularia violacea and Pachyclavularia violacea. Confirmed reclassification to Briareum violaceum. Native distribution: Japan, Taiwan, Marshall Islands and broader Indo-Pacific.
